abstract:
This paper presents the results of a 40-month continuous test campaign evaluating the degradation management of CESAR1—an aqueous blend of 2-amino-2-methylpropan-1-ol (AMP) and piperazine (PZ)—at a lignite-fired power plant pilot facility in Niederaussem. The study challenges conventional industrial guidelines that advocate for maintaining maximum "solvent cleanliness" to minimize amine consumption. Three distinct management strategies were investigated: ion exchange for the removal of ionic contaminants, active carbon adsorption for trace element and color removal, and flue gas pretreatment with thiosulfate/sulfite to reduce $NO_2$ levels.

The findings reveal that standard indicators, such as solvent color and metal concentration, do not reliably predict degradation rates for CESAR1. Notably, while active carbon effectively brightened the solvent and captured iron and nickel, it had no discernible impact on the accumulation of degradation products. Conversely, removing anionic compounds via ion exchange unexpectedly doubled the degradation rate, suggesting that certain accumulated contaminants may actually serve as ligands that deactivate metal catalysts. Furthermore, $NO_2$ removal did not significantly reduce oxidative degradation or nitrosamine formation, though thermal treatment at $130^\circ\text{C}$ proved effective for nitrosamine control without compromising solvent stability. The results underscore the necessity of solvent-specific management strategies and caution against excessive cleaning, which can lead to unnecessary waste and efficiency losses. Over 28,000 operating hours, CESAR1 demonstrated high stability and linear degradation, proving far more robust than traditional monoethanolamine (MEA) systems.
